beats.narration_text: Merhaba — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to write a weekly update for your team. The internal-comms skill fires. First: identify the type. Is this a 3P update, a newsletter, an FAQ, a status report? Then load the matching guideline file. Each file has its own format, tone rules, and tool sources. The skill is a router, not a writer. Let me show you what each type actually requires.

props.command: Write a weekly update for the Product Design team covering last week's progress, plans, and blockers.
output: Format: emoji + team + dates + Progress / Plans / Problems
Constraint: 1-3 sentences per section, data-driven, 30-60s read

beats.narration_text: The skill covers seven communication types, all mapped to four guideline files. 3P updates route to the 3P guide. Company newsletters, FAQ responses, and the rest — status reports, leadership updates, project updates, and incident reports — route to the matching file, or general-comms if nothing else fits. The key rule: the guideline file is authoritative. Never free-style the format. Identify the type first, load the file, then follow its instructions exactly. If the type is unclear, ask for clarification rather than guess.

props.sparkLine: Identify the type. Load the guide. Follow it exactly.

beats.narration_text: The 3P format is the most constrained. Always the same structure: an emoji that captures the team's vibe, the team name, and the dates covered — usually one week. Then three sections. Progress: what shipped, milestones achieved. Plans: what is top-of-mind and high-priority for the next period. Problems: anything slowing the team down. Each section is one to three sentences — clear, to the point, data-driven, metrics where possible. Bigger teams need bigger 3Ps — the company level covers hiring and deals, not individual tasks. Total read time: 30 to 60 seconds. Before writing, confirm the team name.

beats.act: teardown — router insight + what it gets right/bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown moment. The architectural insight of the internal-comms skill is that it is a format router: seven comm types mapped to four guideline files, each with its own format, tone, workflow, and tool sources. Claude does not guess the format — it loads the file. What it gets right: strict 3P structure enforced by the guideline, tool-aware gathering from Slack, email, and calendar, and a fallback to asking when the type is unclear. Where it bites: the guideline files must be populated and maintained by the team; general-comms handles anything that doesn't fit cleanly, which can produce inconsistent output; and the FAQ format relies on reading company-wide sources Claude may not have access to.

props.sparkLine: Router, not writer. The guide is the source of truth.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The internal-comms skill is a format router: 7 comm types, 4 guideline files, each authoritative for its format. The 3P format is the most rigid — same structure every time: emoji, team name, dates, three sections of one to three sentences, data-driven, 30 to 60 second read. FAQ answers pull holistically from Slack, email, and docs. Always identify the type first. Never free-style the format. If the type is unclear, ask.

artifactLines: Format router: 7 comm types → 4 guideline files (load the file, don't guess)
3P: emoji + team + dates + Progress / Plans / Problems — 1-3 sentences each
3P constraint: data-driven, metrics where possible, 30-60 second read
FAQ: Question (1 sentence) + Answer (1-2 sentences) — holistic, not just requester's team
Tool sources: pull from Slack, email, Drive, calendar before drafting
If type unclear: ask — never fall back to free-styling the format

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: Write a 3P update for the Product Design team covering last week. Include progress on the onboarding redesign, plans for user testing, and mention that we are blocked on design system approvals. Use the internal-comms skill. Then watch whether Claude uses the exact 3P format: emoji first, then team name and dates, then three sections — no bullet lists, no prose headers, no more than three sentences per section. If it writes four sentences in the Progress section or skips the emoji, the format constraint was ignored. That is the gate.

props.command: Write a 3P update for the Product Design team covering last week. Progress: onboarding redesign. Plans: user testing. Problems: blocked on design system approvals. Use the internal-comms skill.
props.runningText: Watch: does Claude use the exact 3P format? Emoji first? Max 3 sentences per section?
output: Expected: 🎨 Product Design (Mon Jul 14 – Fri Jul 18)
Expected: Progress / Plans / Problems — each 1-3 sentences, no bullets
Red flag: 4+ sentences per section, missing emoji, or free-form prose layout

beats.narration_text: Internal Comms. Liam, in for Bear.
